title: Why Companies Hate Work From Home Now
description:
Remote work may feel like freedom, but what if it's holding your career back? In this video, Sandeep Swadia former CEO and board member of billion-dollar companies shares 5 brutal truths about remote work — and why it could be the beginning of the end.

From missed promotions and weaker teams to the lost art of mentorship, this deep dive explores the hidden costs of working from home and what top-performing professionals are doing differently.

If you're early in your career or serious about leveling up, this might change the way you think about showing up.
transcript:
Here's the truth about why CEOs hate
remote work. And no, it's not about
productivity. It's something deeper. I
have been the CEO and sat on boards of
billion-dollar companies. And here are
five brutal truths about remote work and
why this might be the beginning of the
end. First up, want to grow faster? Then
stop working from bed and start showing
up. years ago, I used to work on Wall
Street, and I would show up before 6:30
a.m. every day. And my commute was 105
minutes one way, and we got more done
before 9:30 than most people did all
day. And when the markets open at 9:30,
it was war. And I would leave around
midnights most of the nights. Brutal,
sure. But that intensity, that proximity
wasn't just about work. It was about
education. That's where I developed
intuition how businesses work, industry
dynamics, strategy, corporate finance,
M&A. You don't get any of that from an
online course or a webinar. You absorb
it through osmosis, through pressure,
through friction. Right after co I was
working at a large tech company. We were
in New York and I would go to the 47th
floor where we had our favorite coffee
machine and the entire floor would be
completely empty. It was like a ghost
town, like the soul of the company had
left the building. And this is why CEOs
of Amazon, Apple, Salesforce, Starbucks,
Disney, Google, JP Morgan, Goldman
Sachs, all of the CEOs want their
employees back in the office. Working
from home feels so comfortable, right?
But here's the thing, no one grows by
being comfortable. For you to grow your
career, you have to be an apprentice and
learn from a master. It is the oldest
tradition in the world. India had the
guru and shisha tradition. China had the
shifue. Japan had their samurai schools.
And Greece, they literally invented the
word mentor. And none of them required a
Slack account or Zoom calls, did they?
And the data is pretty strong on this
one. Office-based employees develop
skills 28% faster than remote workers.
If you're early in your career or just
hungry to outpace your peers, then find
ways to be uncomfortable. Put yourself
in rooms where the bar is so high that
the only way you can survive is by
raising your own. Next is number two.
Start talking to strangers. I'll tell
you exactly how it happened for me. I
was uh in my late 20s, unemployed, poor
as dirt. So, I would apply to any role
out there. Even it wasn't a great fit. I
was desperate. So I applied to this role
and I got the interview. That interview
was a complete dumpster fire and we both
knew it was not going anywhere. So after
about 30 very awkward minutes, that guy
said, "Well, it's over." I walked into
the company kitchen to grab a cup of sad
coffee and pick up my ego off the floor.
I didn't know anyone there. I had no
plans, no hope, just caffeine and some
damage control. But I struck up a random
conversation with someone at the coffee
machine and we talked for a few minutes
and suddenly something weird happened.
She paused for a second. She looked at
me and said, "Can you just hang around
for a few minutes?" And 5 minutes later,
I'm walking into the CEO's corner
office. Turns out that that random
person at the coffee machine, she was
the CEO's chief of staff. That chance
encounter, just one hallway conversation
in passing turned into my first big
opportunity. I got hired and I reported
directly to the top for the first time
in my life. So, this is what I learned.
Lady luck is not hanging out on Zoom.
She's in the coffee room. And the data
is plentiful on this one, too. Remote
workers are 31% less likely to be
promoted. And you want to hear something
more alarming? 60% of the managers
consider remote workers to be more
replaceable. If you've ever been a
long-distance relationship, remote bonds
break more easily. Here's your move.
Number one, show up to grow up. And
number two, when you do show up, use
your in-person time to build
relationships, not just with your boss,
but with your peers, those who are
junior to you. Be present. Be kind. Be
coachable. Remember, distance creates
doubts. Presence creates promotions.
Moving on to number three. They say
teamwork makes the dream work. It is
true, but only if the team shows up. I
had an interesting experience almost 10
years ago. I was on the board of a
company. They were in the New York area,
but their CEO lived in the Midwest. So,
he would fly in every couple of weeks.
Great guy, great presence, very smart.
But then things got tough for the
company. They missed their numbers for a
couple of quarters. A couple of their
key executives left and suddenly the
board started asking is the CEO close
enough to the team? Is he engaged
enough? Can he really lead the company
from afar? He was the same exact person.
But suddenly we started seeing him
differently because he was the peaceime
leader in a time that required a wartime
presence. We made the hard call to
replace him. And I remember we hated
making that decision. And that's when I
realized that you can't switch on trust
via remote control. Imagine if a
national basketball team said, "Let's
just sync over Zoom, email the playbook
PDF. We'll all practice solo." That
team's not making the playoffs. They're
heading straight to the bottom of the
league. Think about any world class
team, cricket, soccer, F1. They train
together, they win together. Why?
Because team performance isn't about
what you do. is about what all of you do
together. But here is the modern
illusion. Remote work feels like it's
more balanced, but it quietly erodess
the team performance. That's what
worries the CEOs. You don't lose because
people aren't working hard. You lose
because they're not working together as
a team. Teams that stay close build
trust. Watch any worldass basketball
team on the court. It's all
shortorthhand. A quick glance, a quick
pass, a quick gesture. It's all about
rhythm and momentum, and the coach is
right there with them, watching the
plays, guiding them in real time. That's
the speed of trust. Gartner did some
interesting research on this and found
that only one in four remote employees
feel connected to the company. Low
connection leads to low trust leads to
low performance. So, if you want to win
like a championship team, then start
acting like one. show up on the court
and start scoring. Our number four is
about playing the long game. Here's a
great story. In 2014, a company called
AMD was collapsing. Stock was at $3,
losing market share, talent walking out
the door, customers walking with them.
That's when Lisa Sue stepped in as CEO.
Now, she didn't send remote memos from a
mountain retreat. She showed up to the
basement of the fabrication plant. She
met with the customers and rebuilt
trust. She rebuilt the team. She rebuilt
the products. And while the rest of the
world was binging Netflix on weekends,
she show up to work, pushing, planning,
producing, and it worked. Stock jumped
from $3 all the way to 140. Time
magazine called it one of the great
turnaround stories of modern American
business history. And she played the
long game the right way by showing up
and by building relationships that
mattered. That's what it takes to win.
That's why 80% of global CEOs today
believe that remote work will fade
replaced by entire inoff or some kind of
a hybrid structure because leaders today
can't afford to optimize for
convenience. They have to optimize for
performance. That's why Sergey Brin
recently told Googlers that the sweet
spot for them is 60-hour week. The
regular 40-hour work week is not going
to cut it anymore. that won't help the
company stay ahead in this AI arms race.
So, what's your playbook here? Well,
even if you're hybrid, when you show up
in the office, treat your day like an
investment. Be the one who takes
co-workers for coffee. Linger after
meetings, ask real questions. If you
want people to bet on you, then you have
to bet on them first. The real ROI in
business comes from relationships. So,
play the long game. Be nice and you'll
drive a nicer car. Lastly, number five,
let's talk about when remote work does
make sense. First, certain roles are
designed for solo execution, like coding
or QA or legal contracts or copywriting
or video editing or field sales. It's
okay in these situations if you're
remote. But even here, context matters.
a data analyst at a retail brand. Sure,
you can make it work remotely. At a
hedge fund, you need to be at your desk
all the time. Same title, but very
different expectations. Second, some
companies nowadays are built as remote
workplaces from day one. I work with a
couple of startups right now and they
call themselves remote first cultures
and they're doing great. They're
profitable. They're doing 30 million
plus in revenues, killing it. And they
were designed from day one to be remote,
hiring the best talent wherever it
lives. But even there, the entire
company comes together in person at
least twice a year. And the founders,
they work overtime to build culture,
connection, cohesion, trust. So if
you're at one of those companies, you'll
be fine working remotely because it's in
the very fabric of that company's
culture. And the third situation is well
when life happens. Parenthood,
disability, health, relocation. There
are times when remote work isn't an
option. It's a necessity and you have no
choice but to work remotely and that's
totally okay. These are exceptions that
prove the rule. Then there are people
who are exceptions to those exceptions.
A few years ago, we were trying to hire
a CFO, chief financial officer. We met a
dozen candidate and nothing seemed to
click. Then one day someone rolled into
my office. Yes. Rolled in in a
wheelchair. He didn't mention it. We
didn't dwell on it. And he crushed the
interview. He was so strategic, so
seasoned, so articulate, and he had
years of experience as a CFO of a public
company. He was emotionally grounded. We
fell in love with him and we hired him.
And eventually he told me that 20 years
back he had an accident that broke his
spine and he had been in a wheelchair
since. But here's what blew me away. I
worked with him for years, but never
once did I see him arrive or leave the
office. He was always the first one in
and the last one out. And he never asked
if he could work from home, even on
Fridays. Never. He never talked about
resilience and grit perhaps because he
didn't have to. He lived it. And even
now the hair on my hand is standing up
because I'm thinking, how do you even
compete with someone with that kind of
work ethic? It was incredibly
inspiring. Working remotely is always
possible for you. You can make it work.
But if you are really hungry to learn
faster, lead better, or to build deeper
relationships, then remote is not for
you. You want to move up, then you've
got to show up. And if you're truly
ambitious and ready to play the long
game, check out my video on the 20 cheat
codes to truly be successful. Please
subscribe to this channel. Thank you,
and I love you.
